text:
*[Bug 16750] Treat character as factor in summary.default*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16750>

--- Comment #5 from Sebastian Meyer (<mailto:seb.meyer@fau.de|seb.meyer@fau.de>) ---
Coming back to this after some offline discussions, the current proposal for
the default summary for character vectors is (here given as a separate method,
but to be integrated into `summary.default`):

```
summary.character <- function (object, ...)
{
    nas <- <http://is.na|is.na>(object)
    object <- object[!nas]
    ncs <- nchar(object, allowNA = TRUE) # NB: NA if "bytes"-encoded
    nna <- sum(nas)
    value <-
        c(Length    = length(nas),
          N.unique  = length(unique(object)), # NB: not counting any NA value
          N.blank   = length(grep("^[ \t\r\n]*$", object, perl = TRUE)), # NB:
= trimws()
          Min.nchar = min(ncs),
          Max.nchar = max(ncs),
          NAs       = if(nna > 0) nna) # NB: not "NA's" (Bug 18948)
    class(value) <- c("summaryDefault", "table")
    value
}
```

Compared to the earlier draft, this incorporates the following changes:

* `allowNA=TRUE` in `nchar()` ensures that the summary doesn't fail for invalid
or "bytes"-encoded strings but returns NA for "Min.nchar" and "Max.nchar".

* We really want "N.unique" to ignore NAs (whose count is given separately, if
any), so the number of unique values matches the number of levels if summarized
as a factor.

* Switch from "N.empty" to "N.blank": "N.empty" would just count empty strings
(""), but some people guessed it might also count "  " etc. Furthermore, when
producing a summary after `read.table` from manually curated spreadsheet data,
it often happens that some "empty" cells that actually represent missing values
still contain one or more blanks. "Min.nchar" already tells us if there are any
empty strings (which is why I didn't like "N.empty" in the first place), but
the number of "blank" (whitespace-only) elements gives additional information
(this considers platform-independent whitespace similar to the default
`trimws()`). The additional `grep` clearly increases the computational cost
compared to `sum(ncs==0)`, but that should be irrelevant for the typical use of
`summary()` when exploring new datasets with a manageable number of columns.

I plan to have this feature request resolved in the trunk by the end of this
week.

<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16750>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch

--- Comment #6 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
Very nice.  There's one thing which I only note now:
The `NAs` counts are *not* `format()`ted together with the numbers above, which
has been the case for numeric summaries, too.  Only for `factor` they *are*
formatted together and hence properly indented.
With the new character methods, where all the numbers reported are counts, (as
in the factor case), one would really "like" to see the `NAs` counts formatted
together with the other counts.
`summary(penguins_raw)`
is an example which shows all cases (and is also visible in
`tests/Examples/datasets-Ex.Rout` after  `make check-devel` with your new
`summary.character()` in place):

```
> (sDF <- summary(penguins_raw)) |> print(width = 88)
     studyName   Sample Number         Species          Region          Island  
 Length   :344   Min.   :  1.00   Length   :344   Length   :344   Length   :344 
 N.unique :  3   1st Qu.: 29.00   N.unique :  3   N.unique :  1   N.unique :  3 
 N.blank  :  0   Median : 58.00   N.blank  :  0   N.blank  :  0   N.blank  :  0 
 Min.nchar:  7   Mean   : 63.15   Min.nchar: 33   Min.nchar:  6   Min.nchar:  5 
 Max.nchar:  7   3rd Qu.: 95.25   Max.nchar: 41   Max.nchar:  6   Max.nchar:  9 
                 Max.   :152.00                                                 

       Stage       Individual ID Clutch Completion    Date Egg         
 Length   :344   Length   :344   Length   :344     Min.   :2007-11-09  
 N.unique :  1   N.unique :190   N.unique :  2     1st Qu.:2007-11-28  
 N.blank  :  0   N.blank  :  0   N.blank  :  0     Median :2008-11-09  
 Min.nchar: 18   Min.nchar:  4   Min.nchar:  2     Mean   :2008-11-27  
 Max.nchar: 18   Max.nchar:  6   Max.nchar:  3     3rd Qu.:2009-11-16  
                                                   Max.   :2009-12-01  

 Culmen Length (mm) Culmen Depth (mm) Flipper Length (mm) Body Mass (g) 
 Min.   :32.10      Min.   :13.10     Min.   :172.0       Min.   :2700  
 1st Qu.:39.23      1st Qu.:15.60     1st Qu.:190.0       1st Qu.:3550  
 Median :44.45      Median :17.30     Median :197.0       Median :4050  
 Mean   :43.92      Mean   :17.15     Mean   :200.9       Mean   :4202  
 3rd Qu.:48.50      3rd Qu.:18.70     3rd Qu.:213.0       3rd Qu.:4750  
 Max.   :59.60      Max.   :21.50     Max.   :231.0       Max.   :6300  
 NAs    :2          NAs    :2         NAs    :2           NAs    :2     
        Sex      Delta 15 N (o/oo) Delta 13 C (o/oo)      Comments  
 Length   :344   Min.   : 7.632    Min.   :-27.02    Length   :344  
 N.unique :  2   1st Qu.: 8.300    1st Qu.:-26.32    N.unique : 10  
 N.blank  :  0   Median : 8.652    Median :-25.83    N.blank  :  0  
 Min.nchar:  4   Mean   : 8.733    Mean   :-25.69    Min.nchar: 18  
 Max.nchar:  6   3rd Qu.: 9.172    3rd Qu.:-25.06    Max.nchar: 68  
 NAs      :11    Max.   :10.025    Max.   :-23.79    NAs      :290  
                 NAs    :14        NAs    :13                       
> 
```
And it's the last group (and 2nd-to-last line above) where the formatting (i.e.
non-indentation of `NAs`) of the `Sex` summary is unfortunate and the one for
`Comments` is entirely "lucky".

Note however that this really the  `summary.data.frame()` method which needs
tweaking; it produces a character _matrix_ which then is simply `print()`ed
above.
Hence, all the above should not affect the discussion here about "the optimal" 
`summary(<character>)` method.
Actually, the now **unified** use of `"NAs"` could / should be made use of in
tweaking  `summary.data.frame()`.

text:
*[Bug 18992] New: please support tweaking LDFLAGS used for pkg-config file*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18992>

            Bug ID: 18992
           Summary: please support tweaking LDFLAGS used for pkg-config
                    file
           Product: R
           Version: R-devel (trunk)
          Hardware: All
                OS: Linux
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Installation
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:bdrung@debian.org|bdrung@debian.org>

When building the pkg-config file for libR in `src/unix/Makefile.in`, the
following stanza,

```
install-pc:
    @$(MKINSTALLDIRS) "$(DESTDIR)$(libdir)/pkgconfig"
    -@$(SED) -e "s:@rhome:$(rhome):" \
      -e "s:@rincludedir:$(rincludedir):" \
      -e "s:@rarch:$(R_ARCH):" \
      -e 's:@libsprivate:$(STATIC_LIBR_PC):' \
      -e 's:@others:$(MAIN_LDFLAGS) $(LDFLAGS):' \
      -e 's/@VERSION/$(VERSION)/' $(srcdir)/libR.pc.in \
      > "$(DESTDIR)$(libdir)/pkgconfig/libR.pc"
```

combines `MAIN_LDFLAGS` with `LDFLAGS` and sticks it into the "Libs" field of
the resulting `libR.pc` file. That's not what you want: `LDFLAGS` contains the
user's linker flags at this point, i.e. whatever they have in their environment
variable.

This has been reported as bug #17744 before and Gentoo removes the `LDFLAGS`
with a patch. On Ubuntu we started setting --package-metadata and ran into
<https://launchpad.net/bugs/2138363>. The --package-metadata flag must not leak
into the pkg-config file.

So please allow distribution packagers to tweaking LDFLAGS used for pkg-config
file.

text:
*[Bug 18993] New: do not ignore failure to create libR.pc*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18993>

            Bug ID: 18993
           Summary: do not ignore failure to create libR.pc
           Product: R
           Version: R-devel (trunk)
          Hardware: All
                OS: Linux
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Installation
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:bdrung@debian.org|bdrung@debian.org>

Silently failing to create `libR.pc` will make debugging harder and is not what
downstream distributions want.

The sed command can fail in case one of the substitution strings contains the
separator. Instead of ignoring the failure, a separator character should be
picked that is not part of the substitution string. `LDFLAGS` might contain `:`
and fail.

text:
*[Bug 17136] Documentation for method="BY" in p.adjust()*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=17136>

Mahjabin Oyshi (<mailto:oyshimahjabin3@gmail.com|oyshimahjabin3@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |oyshimahjabin3@gmail.com

--- Comment #2 from Mahjabin Oyshi (<mailto:oyshimahjabin3@gmail.com|oyshimahjabin3@gmail.com>) ---
Created attachment 3600
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3600&action=edit>
Clarify BY method documentation (harmonic correction and conservativeness).

This patch updates the documentation of the BY option in p.adjust(). The
previous text grouped BH and BY together and said they were ‘more powerful than
the others’, which is not accurate for BY. The new wording separates BH and BY,
briefly describes how BY modifies BH to handle general dependence, and
clarifies that BY is much more conservative than BH and can even be more
conservative than Bonferroni

elements.text:
) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |oyshimahjabin3@gmail.com

--- Comment #2 from Mahjabin Oyshi (

Clarify BY method documentation (harmonic correction and conservativeness).

This patch updates the documentation of the BY option in p.adjust(). The
previous text grouped BH and BY together and said they were ‘more powerful than
the others’, which is not accurate for BY. The new wording separates BH and BY,
briefly describes how BY modifies BH to handle general dependence, and
clarifies that BY is much more conservative than BH and can even be more
conservative than Bonferroni

<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=17136>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
     Ever confirmed|0                           |1
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CONFIRMED
                 CC|                            |maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch

--- Comment #3 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
Thank you, so now we got a 3rd iteration of John Maindonald's soon 10 year old
proposal.   I assume R core has remained quiet for so long, because none of us
felt expert enough in the field to judge the different nuances.
The latest patch (Comment 2) uses somewhat too strong language I think
("substantially" / "often"), also separates "BH" from "BY" such that they are
described less jointly.NN


JM's example showing that BY may be more conservative than even Bonferroni and
hence is important to simply show why the current phrasing needs correction.
It also does mention "Theorem 1.3" (as patch #2) which I want to keep (as from
JM's original patch).

I plan to commit my "mixture" of your propositions within the next two days.

text:
*[Bug 16750] Treat character as factor in summary.default*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16750>

--- Comment #8 from Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) ---
I think this is good. It is very similar to what we ended up with for
characters in skimr. We include more by default, but that doesn't make sense in
the context of `summary()`. As mentioned,for example, we do count the number of
whitespace strings

I think this proposal is good for base R, and the number of statistics is
similar to that for other classes. And then it's up to the user to decide if
the character vector should really be a factor vector.
